title: Pega Platform '25 — Low-Code Application Development Complete Guide
content:
# Pega Platform '25 — Low-Code Application Development

## Overview
Pega Platform '25 enables low-code and user-friendly application development. With Pega Platform, you can deliver flexible applications that help customers reach business goals in dynamically changing scenarios. The platform supports planning flexible Microjourneys, adopting feature-driven development, reusing built-on capabilities, and engaging stakeholders through collaborative tools.

## Authoring Portals (Studios)

Pega Platform offers four authoring portals, each targeted at different roles:

### App Studio
**Purpose**: Low-code, user-friendly application development for business analysts, citizen developers, front-end developers, and data engineers.
**Portal**: pxExpress
**Key capabilities**:
- Create and manage Case Types (reusable business process templates)
- Define Personas (user groups with specific access settings)
- Configure Data Objects for case data
- Design Microjourneys (customer success paths)
- Real-time UI design — anyone in the business process can contribute to planning and development
- Manage Channels (email, chat bot, web, mobile)
- Access Reuse Library for managing reusable Case Types across applications
- Developer Assistant pane with Guidance, Knowledge, Work, and Checklists tabs
- Pega Autopilot (GenAI) integration for AI-assisted development
- Pega Coach for automated user guidance at runtime
- Accessibility Inspector, Clipboard, and Tracer tools
- Agile Workbench integration for work tracking

**App Studio work areas**: Overview, Case Types, Personas, Channels, Data & Interfaces, UI, Library, Settings

### Dev Studio
**Purpose**: Advanced application configuration for experienced developers, security admins, and account admins.
**Portal**: pxStudio (Developer)
**Key capabilities**:
- Advanced Rule forms and Rules management
- Complex business rules configuration
- Creating reusable functionalities for App Studio
- Complex integration and data page configurations
- Security, versioning, and source control management
- Branch development and merging
- Application Definition management
- Ruleset stack configuration
- Compliance score monitoring

**Best practice**: Perform as much work as possible in App Studio. Switch to Dev Studio for complex configurations and advanced solutions.

### Admin Studio
**Purpose**: System monitoring and resource management for system administrators.
**Portal**: pxAdminStudio
**Key capabilities**:
- Monitor and debug system resources (nodes, agents, queues)
- View system overview and resource statistics
- Start, stop, and manage resources
- Access Background Process management
- Monitor application performance

### Prediction Studio
**Purpose**: AI and machine learning model lifecycle management for data scientists.
**Portal**: pxPredictionStudio
**Five work areas**: Predictions, Models, Data, Reports, Settings
**Key capabilities**:
- Create and manage Predictions (Case Management, Customer Decision Hub, Text Analytics)
- Develop and manage Adaptive and Predictive Models
- Manage predictor fields, Data Sets, taxonomies, sentiment lexicons
- MLOps — replace models, approve updates for production deployment
- Machine learning services integration (AWS SageMaker, Google Vertex AI)
- Monitoring and notifications for model performance
- Read-only in Production environment by default (safety feature)

### Switching Between Studios
- Click the Switch Studio menu in the header of your current studio
- You only see studios relevant to your role (based on Access Group portal associations)
- Navigation: Header > Studio Name dropdown > Select target studio

## App Studio vs Dev Studio — Naming Differences

When you create resources in App Studio, Pega creates one or more Rules and potentially classes. Dev Studio provides visibility into all created records while App Studio exposes only the user-facing elements.

| App Studio Term | Dev Studio Term |
|---|---|
| Case Type | Case Type Rule + associated classes |
| Stage | Stage shape in Case Lifecycle |
| Process | Flow Rule |
| Step | Assignment, Automation, or Decision shape |
| Persona | Access Group |
| Channel | Channel interface Rule |
| Data Object | Data Type + Data Page |
| View | Section Rule |

## Building Your First Application

### Application Creation Process
1. **Define users and goals**: Identify who will use the application and how
2. **Design in Pega Blueprint**: Sketch the minimum lovable product (MLP) using GenAI-powered Blueprint
3. **Import into Pega Platform**: Import Blueprint design using New Application wizard
4. **Set up development**: Prepare user stories, customize structure and components
5. **Develop collaboratively**: Add collaborators (run-time users and design-time developers)
6. **Configure security**: Complete the Application Security Checklist before deployment
7. **Deploy**: Ensure all security requirements are met before going live

### Creating an Application from Pega Blueprint
- Design application structure in Pega Blueprint (GenAI-powered)
- Download the Blueprint design
- In Pega Platform, use the New Application wizard to import the Blueprint
- System auto-creates Case Types, Stages, Personas, Data Objects based on the Blueprint
- Continue development by completing tasks in Agile Workbench

### Exporting an Application as a Blueprint
- Export an existing Pega application back to Blueprint format
- Useful for redesigning or sharing application designs

### Developer Assistant Pane
Four tabs for development guidance:
1. **Guidance**: Context-sensitive suggestions and best practices based on your current work
2. **Knowledge**: Documentation and reference materials
3. **Work**: Integration with Agile Workbench for task tracking
4. **Checklists**: Pre-configured checklists including the Application Security Checklist

## Creating a Microjourney for Customer Success

### What is a Microjourney?
A Microjourney is a unit of work that results in a particular outcome within a customer journey. A journey is the series of interactions between a customer and an organization as the customer pursues a specific goal (e.g., hiring a job candidate). Microjourneys break the journey into manageable units (e.g., reviewing a job application).

### Microjourney Foundation Elements
1. **Case Types**: Visualize the path of your business processes
2. **Personas**: Represent people involved in processes and the Channels they use
3. **Data**: Information that processes require to reach resolution

### Planning a Microjourney
- Create draft relationships between Cases, Personas, and Data Objects
- Estimate time and effort needed to turn drafts into permanent elements
- In App Studio, visualize main elements: people involved, communication channels, and required data
- Include non-technical stakeholders in the planning process

### Application Update Scenarios for Microjourneys
- Updates to built-on applications propagate to dependent applications
- System creates Personas for all Access Groups that don't have studio access
- Releases are matched with color-coded symbols

## Adding Case Types to Organize Work

### What is a Case Type?
A Case Type is a reusable template for a business process that users complete at runtime to reach a successful outcome. Each time a user starts a new transaction, Pega creates a Case (specific instance) of the Case Type.

### Case Lifecycle Structure
- **Stages**: Major phases of the business process (e.g., Submission, Review, Approval)
- **Processes**: Sequential or parallel groups of actions within a Stage
- **Steps**: Individual actions within a Process (Assignments, Automations, Decisions)

### Creating a Top-Level Case Type
1. In App Studio navigation, click Case Types
2. Click New or Add a Case Type
3. Enter Case Type name, select Type: Standard
4. Optional: Reuse assets from another Case Type (Advanced option)
5. Click Next — system creates default Create Stage with Create Process

### Creating a Primary Stage
- Stages represent main phases (e.g., Submission, Application review, Decision, Job offer)
- Navigate: App Studio > Case Types > [Case Type] > Workflow > Lifecycle
- Click Stage to add a new stage, enter unique descriptive name
- Configure transition: Automatically move to next Stage, Wait for user action, or Resolve the Case
- **Best practice**: Use descriptive, business-oriented Stage names that stakeholders can understand
- The Create Stage (green bar) is auto-created; Resolution Stage (red bar) marks the end

### Adding Processes to Stages
- Sequential Process: Orders related actions (e.g., Prepare questions > Conduct interview > Prepare assessment)
- Navigate: Case Type > Workflow > Lifecycle > Stage > More > Add Process
- Can add new Process or reuse existing Process
- Drag processes to change runtime order

### Adding Steps to Processes
- Steps are individual actions: Assignments (human tasks), Automations (system actions), Decisions (routing logic)
- Configure each step with specific routing, SLAs, and conditions

### Case Type Best Practices
- Create parent-child Case hierarchies for complex processes
- Define routing for assignments (Work Queue, Worklist, specific worker, or automatic routing)
- Set SLAs with escalation actions for timely resolution
- Design surveys for collecting feedback from end users
- Use Pega Blueprint for optimal Case Type design suggestions

## Adding Personas to Organize Users

### What is a Persona?
Personas represent groups of users that interact with your application. They define what content is appropriate for each user type. Personas map to Access Groups in the Pega security model.

### Persona Types
- **Run-time Personas**: End users who process cases (e.g., Applicant, Underwriter, Customer Service Rep, Manager)
- **Design-time Personas**: Developers and admins who build the application (e.g., Developer, Administrator)

### Creating Personas
- Navigate: App Studio > Personas > New
- Define access settings for each group
- Associate Personas with Case Types (who can work on which cases)
- Associate Personas with Channels (how they interact)
- Grant Personas access to specific Channels and pages
- Configure access options (which portals, which Case Types)

### Creating Personas with AI
- Pega Autopilot can suggest Personas based on your application structure
- AI analyzes Case Types and Channels to recommend appropriate user groups

## Adding Data Objects to Organize Data

### What are Data Objects?
Data Objects organize the data that Cases need to reach resolution. Instead of storing separate data objects for each piece of personal information (name, surname, phone), gather them in a single personal details Data Object.

### Data Object Configuration
- Associate Data Objects with Case Types
- Implement draft Data Objects into permanent configurations
- Associate attachment categories with Case Types
- Data Objects map to Data Types and Data Pages in Dev Studio

## Application Development Considerations

Before starting development, consider:
1. **Business goal**: Center development around the goal, create agile applications that adjust to changing needs
2. **People in your process**: Determine user types, define appropriate content and access for each
3. **Data requirements**: What information to collect, how to gather it, how to organize and reuse data
4. **Reusable elements**: Identify assets that can be reused across applications (banking operations reused in mortgage and loan apps)
5. **Channels and devices**: Email, chat bot, web portal, mobile — what communication channels do users need?
6. **Roles**: Define the organizational roles and map them to Personas

## Feature-Driven Development

### What is Feature-Driven Development?
Develop capabilities in the context of features to maintain functional requirements and project status. Associate features with application elements (e.g., language pack). Use features to estimate project costs and schedules before development.

### Feature Management
- **Features**: Top-level functional requirements (correspond to Case Types and Data Types)
- **Subfeatures**: Breakdown of features (correspond to Stages within Case Types)
- **Feature order**: Priority and sequence of implementation
- **Feature levels**: Complexity classification (Low, Medium, High)
- Track progress with associated user stories, bugs, and feedback items

### Agile Workbench
- Built-in work management tool for tracking development
- Create and manage stories, bugs, and feedback items
- Set current work items, change statuses
- Collaborate with development teams
- Integrate with Pega Agile Studio or Jira for advanced project management

## Designing Applications for Reuse and Extension

### Built-on Applications
Reuse functionality from built-on applications to focus development on unique features. Example: Banking operations app reused in Mortgage requests and Loan requests apps.

**Application stack hierarchy**: Your app > Built-on apps > PegaRULES (base)

### Best Practices for Multiple Built-on Applications
- Avoid duplicating Rules across applications
- Plan your application stack hierarchy carefully
- Use the Reuse Library to manage shared Case Types and assets

### Reuse Library
- Manage reusable Case Types, data types, and other assets across applications
- Author and edit reusable assets
- Mark records as 'relevant' in Dev Studio to make them available in App Studio

### Components
- Install, enable, and disable components from Pega Marketplace or custom-built
- Package components into shared Rulesets for distribution
- Document components for team collaboration

### Configuration Sets
- Organize configurable business logic that business users can modify
- Create Configurations and decision trees within Configuration Sets
- Add Administration landing pages for business user access

## Building Logic and Calculating Values

### Activities
- Step-by-step procedural logic (traditional programming approach)
- Configure steps with method parameters and preconditions
- Define step transitions and testing
- Unit test activities; use Tracer for debugging
- **Best practice**: Use activities for background jobs; prefer declarative rules and data transforms for foreground logic
- Security tab controls execution permissions
- Local variables for temporary data storage
- Create activities for Process automation (intelligent routing)

### Decision Tables
- Evaluate conditions in rows to return a result from a matrix of values
- Configure pages, classes, and additional options
- Calculate values in App Studio using decision tables
- Unit test and debug with Tracer

### Decision Trees
- Hierarchical branching logic for complex decision-making
- Configure property evaluation at branch nodes
- Control output and actions at each branch
- Unit test and debug with Tracer

### Declare Expression Rules
- Automatically compute property values when inputs change
- Define expressions, specify pages and classes
- **Change Tracking tab**: Configure forward chaining (eager) vs backward chaining (lazy)
- View relationships in the Declarative Network
- Test in the Declarative Network Viewer

### Declare OnChange Rules
- Monitor an entire page for any property change
- Execute activities when changes are detected
- Configure Properties tab and Pages & Classes tab
- Implement business exception processing with policy overrides

### Declare Trigger Rules
- Execute an activity when a specific property changes
- Configure monitored property, trigger event, and target activity
- Pages and Classes tab defines scope

### Map Values
- Map data between source and target properties
- Complete Matrix tab, Configuration tab, and Pages & Classes tab
- Unit test map values

### When Condition Rules
- Evaluate Boolean conditions (true/false)
- Use Condition Builder for visual condition design
- Configure advanced options (AND/OR logic)
- Unit test and debug with Tracer

### Validations
- Ensure data integrity through validation rules
- On-demand validation at specific workflow points

### Expression Builder
- Build complex expressions using property references, functions, and constants
- Available functions organized by category
- Use in declare expressions, when conditions, and other rule types

### Constraints Rules
- Always-on validation that evaluates whenever data changes
- Configure constraints on the Constraints tab and Pages & Classes tab
- Distinct from validate rules (continuous vs on-demand)

## Developing Applications in Branches

### What are Branches?
Branches provide separate development sandboxes to avoid conflicts between developers working on the same application.

### Branch Development Workflow
1. **Create a development application** with branched development enabled
2. **Add development Branches** for each developer or feature
3. **Develop in isolation** — each Branch has its own Ruleset
4. **Review Branches** — create Branch reviews for peer review
5. **Merge Branches** — merge completed work into the target Ruleset
6. **Resolve conflicts** — handle any merge conflicts

### Branch Operations
- Set Branch development preferences
- Rule resolution with Branch development preferences
- View Branch quality and contents (quality metrics)
- Reorder, lock, package, remove, and delete Branches

### Branch Reviews
- Create, modify, close, and reopen reviews
- Review Branch contents before merging

### Merging Branches
- Use the Merge Branches wizard
- Handle conflicts and warnings
- Customize the Branch merge process

### Branches and Rulesets
- Branches create Branch Rulesets
- Unlocked Rulesets vs Branch Rulesets
- Develop in Workspaces for additional isolation

## Security for Public-Facing Applications

### Security Checklist
Pega provides a comprehensive Security Checklist with minimum requirements:
- Follow best practices for secure deployment
- Ensure confidentiality, integrity, and availability
- Track task completion on Dev Studio home page
- Complete before deploying any public-facing application

### Authentication Types
- **Anonymous user**: Identity unknown initially, can register or authenticate later (e.g., online shopping)
  - Authentication Service of Anonymous Type
  - Access Group for anonymous users with limited access
- **Authenticated user**: Credentials verified at login via external data store
  - Authentication Service of Basic Credentials type
  - Verify credentials using external identity store + Data Page
- **Single sign-on (SSO)**: Authenticate via external identity provider (e.g., Google)
  - SAML, OAuth, OIDC authentication services

### Access Control
- Restrict access to Data-Admin-Operator-ID class in public-facing apps
- Configure Access Groups with appropriate portal access
- Use Access of Role to Object (ARO) rules for fine-grained control

## Compliance and Quality

### Compliance Score
- Monitor application health with the compliance score
- Analyze individual warnings and their impact
- Resolve or justify warnings
- Schedule compliance reports for ongoing monitoring
- Review UI components for accessibility

### Unit Testing
- Test individual Rules in isolation
- Each rule type (activity, decision table, decision tree, when rule, map value) has unit testing support
- View Clipboard pages created during tests

## Troubleshooting Tools and Techniques

### Tracer
- Real-time debugging tool for tracing rule execution
- Configure trace conditions and Tracer settings
- Set breakpoints and watch variables
- Select specific sessions to trace
- Trace events: all events, specific event types, specific rulesets
- View results in the Tracer window
- Send output to file for offline analysis with Pega-TracerViewer
- **Best practices**: Reduce event output, adjust buffer size, manage disk usage

### Clipboard Tool
- Inspect runtime data on the Clipboard (pages, properties, values)
- Set property values for testing
- Add user pages for experimentation
- Measure Clipboard size for memory management
- Troubleshoot long-lived Clipboard pages
- View pages created by unit tests

### Performance Analyzer (PAL)
- Analyze application performance
- Identify slow rules, database queries, and bottlenecks
- Monitor response times and resource usage

### My Alerts Display
- Parse and summarize alert logs
- Suppress sensitive data in alerts
- Understand alert format and severity levels

### Additional Tools
- View and resolve errors
- View in-progress and completed wizards
- Create connector simulators for testing integrations
- DateTime parse tester tool
- Bulk Revalidate and Save tool
- Application Structure landing page (Ruleset Stack, Prerequisites)

## Delivering Application Documentation

### Documentation Tools
- Create project documents for stakeholders in Dev Studio
- Export Data Model into documents
- Generate application overview documents
- Customize document style and structure
- Create guides for application developers and administrators

### Guided Tours
- Describe features to end users with guided tours
- Create content for guided tours
- Associate content with features
- Integrate guided tours with your application

## Interview & Certification Q&A

**Q: What are the four authoring portals in Pega Platform and who uses each?**
A: (1) App Studio (pxExpress) — for citizen developers, business analysts, front-end developers; low-code UI for Case Types, Personas, Channels, Data Objects. (2) Dev Studio (pxStudio) — for experienced developers and security admins; advanced Rules, versioning, branching, complex integrations. (3) Admin Studio (pxAdminStudio) — for system administrators; monitor/manage nodes, agents, background processes. (4) Prediction Studio (pxPredictionStudio) — for data scientists; manage AI/ML model lifecycle, predictions, adaptive models.

**Q: What is a Microjourney and what are its foundation elements?**
A: A Microjourney is a unit of work within a customer journey that results in a particular outcome. Its three foundation elements are: Case Types (business process templates), Personas (user groups and their channels), and Data (information processes need). You plan Microjourneys in App Studio by creating draft relationships between these elements.

**Q: What is the difference between App Studio and Dev Studio?**
A: App Studio is for low-code development — Case Type design, data modeling, UI/UX, channel management, basic business rules. Dev Studio is for advanced configuration — complex Rules, creating reusable functionalities, complex integrations, data page configurations, security management, branching. Best practice: do as much as possible in App Studio, use Dev Studio for complex configurations.

**Q: How do Branches work in Pega application development?**
A: Branches provide isolated development sandboxes. Each developer or feature gets its own Branch with a separate Branch Ruleset. Developers work in isolation, then merge back into the target Ruleset. The workflow is: create Branch > develop > review (peer review) > merge > resolve conflicts. Branch quality metrics help assess code quality before merging.

**Q: What is Pega Blueprint and how does it integrate with application development?**
A: Pega Blueprint is a GenAI-powered tool for designing application structures. You sketch your minimum lovable product (MLP) in Blueprint, defining Case Types, Stages, Personas, and Data Objects. Then import the Blueprint into Pega Platform using the New Application wizard, which auto-creates the application structure. You can also export existing applications back to Blueprint format.

**Q: What tools does Pega provide for debugging and troubleshooting?**
A: (1) Tracer — real-time rule execution tracing with breakpoints and watch variables. (2) Clipboard tool — inspect runtime data, pages, and property values. (3) PAL (Performance Analyzer) — analyze response times and identify bottlenecks. (4) My Alerts — parse and summarize alert logs. (5) Compliance score — monitor application health. (6) Unit testing — test individual Rules (activities, decision tables, decision trees, when rules). (7) Connector simulators — test integrations without live endpoints.

**Q: What is a Case Type and how is it structured?**
A: A Case Type is a reusable template for a business process. Structure: Stages (major phases like Submission, Review, Approval) > Processes (sequential or parallel groups of actions within a Stage) > Steps (individual actions — Assignments for human tasks, Automations for system actions, Decisions for routing logic). Each Case instance created at runtime is a specific transaction of the Case Type.

**Q: What security considerations apply to public-facing Pega applications?**
A: Complete the Security Checklist before deployment. Key areas: (1) Authentication — configure Anonymous, Basic Credentials, or SSO authentication services. (2) Access control — restrict Data-Admin-Operator-ID access, configure Access Groups. (3) Data protection — protect sensitive data, suppress in alerts. (4) Branding — avoid exposing server logos/information. Do NOT deploy without meeting all Pega security requirements.

**Q: What is the Reuse Library and how does it work?**
A: The Reuse Library in App Studio manages reusable Case Types and assets across multiple applications. Assets created in Dev Studio can be marked as 'relevant records' to make them available in App Studio. This promotes a modular approach: create building blocks in Dev Studio, assemble them in App Studio. Benefits include faster development, engaging more people with appropriate tools for their roles.
